name: Israfil
description: Israfil, also known as Sarafiel, is an archangel revered in Islam. He is primarily known for his role as the angel who will signal the Day of Judgment by blowing the trumpet, an event described in the Quran and elaborated upon in Islamic traditions. Depictions of Israfil often portray him as a towering figure with immense wings and a trumpet perpetually held to his lips, awaiting God's command. While not explicitly mentioned in the Quran by name, interpretations link him to references of angels tasked with specific duties related to the end times. His significance lies in his eschatological role, embodying the divine power and the inevitability of resurrection and judgment. Sunni traditions often place him among the four major archangels, alongside Jibril (Gabriel), Mika'il (Michael), and Azrael. Accounts of his creation vary, but he is generally understood to be among the first angels created by God.
subtitle: The Trumpeter of Doom

keyMyths.title: The Blowing of the Trumpet
keyMyths.description: Israfil's most significant role is blowing the trumpet to signal the Day of Judgment. The first blow will bring about the destruction of the world and the death of all living beings. A second blow will then resurrect all souls for judgment before God. This event is a cornerstone of Islamic eschatology and serves as a reminder of accountability for one's actions in life. The precise timing and nature of these blows are subjects of theological discussion and interpretation.

keyMyths.title: Israfil's Constant Vigil
keyMyths.description: It is believed that Israfil stands eternally before God, his trumpet poised and ready, awaiting the divine command to initiate the Day of Judgment. This constant state of readiness highlights the unwavering obedience of angels to God's will and the imminence of the end times. Some accounts describe Israfil's eyes constantly fixed upon the Divine Throne, awaiting the signal.

keyMyths.title: Israfil's Role in Creation
keyMyths.description: Some traditions suggest Israfil played a role in the creation of the universe and the recording of events. He is sometimes associated with the celestial music and the harmony of the spheres, reflecting the divine order and beauty of creation. This role connects him to the broader cosmic narrative beyond his eschatological function. His role in creation, however, is less emphasized compared to his role in the end times.

sources.author: Wheeler, Brannon M.

sources.description: Provides context for understanding the roles of angels in Islamic theology and Quranic interpretation.
sources.text: Provides context for understanding the roles of angels in Islamic theology and Quranic interpretation.

sources.text: While focusing on Jihad, the book provides relevant information on Islamic eschatology and the Day of Judgment.

associations.name: Trumpet
associations.significance: Symbolizes the announcement of the Day of Judgment and resurrection.

associations.name: Resurrection
associations.significance: Israfil's trumpet blast initiates the resurrection of the dead.

associations.name: Four
associations.significance: Associated with the four archangels in Islamic tradition.

associations.name: Air
associations.significance: Connects to the sound of the trumpet echoing through the heavens.

associations.name: Divine Command
associations.significance: Represents obedience to God's will and the execution of His decrees.

associations.name: Heavens
associations.significance: Israfil's position as an archangel places him in the celestial realm, close to God.

worshipPractices.name: Remembrance of Death and Judgment
worshipPractices.description: While Israfil is not directly worshipped, contemplating his role in the Day of Judgment encourages Muslims to reflect on their mortality and the importance of righteous living. Remembering Israfil's trumpet call serves as a reminder of accountability before God.

cultural.modernLegacy: Israfil's image persists in Islamic art, literature, and sermons as a powerful symbol of the end times. He serves as a reminder of divine judgment and the importance of leading a righteous life in anticipation of the Day of Resurrection. The concept of Israfil blowing the trumpet frequently appears in discussions about Islamic eschatology.

extendedContent.title: Origins & Birth
extendedContent.content: Israfil's origins are rooted in Islamic tradition, although parallels can be drawn to angelology in other Abrahamic faiths. While the Quran mentions the trumpet blast associated with the Day of Judgment, it doesn't explicitly name Israfil as the angel responsible. Islamic scholars and commentators have identified him through various hadiths (sayings and actions of Prophet Muhammad). His creation, like that of other angels, is believed to predate humanity. He is often described as one of the four major archangels in Islam, alongside Jibril (Gabriel), Mika'il (Michael), and Azrael (the angel of death). Some accounts detail Israfil's constant gaze upon the Divine Throne, awaiting God's command to sound the trumpet. His existence is entirely devoted to serving God's will and executing His divine plan concerning the end of the world.

extendedContent.title: Major Myths & Stories
extendedContent.content: The central myth surrounding Israfil revolves around his role in signaling the Day of Judgment. Islamic eschatology describes two trumpet blasts: the first will bring about the destruction of the world, and the second will resurrect all beings for judgment. Israfil will sound both trumpets upon God's command. He is often depicted holding a massive trumpet, ready to fulfill his divinely ordained task. Some traditions recount that Israfil was sent by God, along with Jibril and Mika'il, to collect soil from the earth to create Adam. However, the earth pleaded with them not to take from it, and only Israfil succeeded in bringing back the soil. This story highlights Israfil's unwavering obedience to God, even when faced with emotional appeals.

extendedContent.title: Legacy & Influence
extendedContent.content: Israfil's legacy is primarily tied to Islamic eschatology and the concept of divine judgment. He serves as a potent reminder of the transient nature of earthly life and the importance of preparing for the afterlife. His image is frequently invoked in sermons, religious literature, and artistic representations of the Day of Judgment. The sound of the trumpet is a powerful symbol, evoking both fear and hope – fear of divine retribution for the wicked and hope of paradise for the righteous. Israfil's role also underscores the Islamic belief in the absolute sovereignty of God and the obedience of angels to His commands. He reminds believers of their ultimate accountability and encourages them to strive for moral and spiritual excellence.

shortDescription: Israfil is the archangel in Islam who will signal the Day of Judgment by blowing the trumpet, initiating resurrection and divine judgment.
